body_txt: Imagine you found yourself Trapped in Another World , far removed from everything you know with no promise you could ever go back. And just as you started to come to grips with that, you discovered you were The Chosen One and that this world depended on you. And then your band of quirky companions , who had been your support, started to disappear one by one , leaving you alone with The Mole . And then the person you thought was your last friend betrayed you to the Big Bad , who threw you into his dungeon and promised to execute you at dawn. As you sit there in your chains, how do you feel? Well, fortunately for you, you're a fictional character, so you get right to work on befriending the magical talking mice who know where the keys are kept . Turns out they know where to find a cool enchanted sword, too! This is awesome! Angst? What angst? The polar opposite of Wangst , this is when a character has been given every reason to burst into tears or indulge in a nice long scream, and... just doesn't, and life goes on. They aren't holding off despair through sheer willpower . Nor are they putting on a brave face because they don't want anyone feeling sorry for them . They're just sort of rolling with it, riding out the adventure as it comes and looking ahead to the next plot event. This appears frequently in children's media, particularly adventure stories featuring young heroes who never Freak Out when piloting a burning biplane into a T. rex's gaping maw. These protagonists take everything in stride. If anything, they think it's all impossibly cool and wish it would never end. Actual children would be forgiven if they burst into hysterics, but it isn't fun for actual children to read about fictional kids screaming in terror as their lives fall apart. Similarly, child characters will bounce back from injury or trauma more easily than real children would, since actual children aren't always able to tell fantasy from reality: the characters are people to them, and they can feel stricken for days at the thought of their favorite 'person' suffering. So fictional children don't. That said, it doesn't have to be an adventure story or a work specifically aimed at the younger set: any genre that puts characters through the emotional wringer is a good environment for this trope (except the ones that thrive on the cast overemoting , such as any melodrama and particularly Soap Opera ). Given how disliked Wangst is, you'd think this would be preferable, but less is not always more . A fictional character shrugging at trauma that would reduce a Real Life person to emotional collapse — or at least a few honest tears — can definitely strain the audience's Willing Suspension of Disbelief (and disturb the in-universe characters too). Someone who does not react to suffering at all can come across as cruel and uncaring — hardly what it's meant to express. Of course, it can be used for laughs as well, or even for drama, especially if the lack of any emotion is used to imply that something is seriously "off" with the character in question. If it's later revealed you were holding back the tears while chatting with those talking, key-fetching mice, then that's Cerebus Retcon , and it overlaps with Salvaged Story if the retcon were done in response to complaints about your original lack of emotion. Possibly Truth in Television for the more extreme cases. If you're fighting for your life, you don't have time to think about how awful it is — not at that very second, anyway. Afterward, all bets are off. Also in some cases, it could be presumed that mourning did happen, just offscreen. Also truth in television in that someone experiencing a potentially traumatic situation does not automatically mean that they consequently will be traumatised; people can vary in terms of mental resilience. According to the National Centre for PTSD , while most people will experience a potentially traumatising event at least once in their lives, only about 6 out of 100 people will go on to actually develop PTSD as a result. So while Real Life humans might not be have the plot-powered resilience of their fictional counterparts, they apparently can be very good at shrugging off or adapting to bad circumstances. Contrast The Stoic , Conditioned to Accept Horror , Too Broken to Break , Seen It All , and Unfazed Everyman for when a character's lack of angst is justified by their personality or past experiences; and The Sociopath and Lack of Empathy for when they're treated as wrong or callous for their indifference. Also contrast Emotion Suppression and Heroic Safe Mode , where a character can't feel anything right at the moment; and Stepford Smiler and Tough Leader Façade , when a character feels pain but doesn't dare show it because of their social role. Related to the Law of Disproportionate Response , where underreactions to big events (or an overreaction to something petty) shows the character to be mentally unstable, for comedy or for drama. Compare Plot-Powered Stamina , where characters shrug off physical (rather than emotional) trauma and stress like it's nothing. See also Blissfully Horrific Backstory . In many examples of Merchandising the Monster , this seems to be present.
